column2: Question
column3: Answer

column2: What is the QTAC code for the standalone Bachelor of Information Technology?
column3: The QTAC code for the Bachelor of Information Technology is 733001. Domestic students, including interstate applicants, apply through QTAC at qtac.edu.au.

column2: What was the median entry score for BIT at UQ for Semester 1, 2026?
column3: The median entry score for the Bachelor of Information Technology (Semester 1, 2026) was 87.4, with a minimum of 81.9 and a highest of 95.45. Meeting the minimum does not guarantee admission.

column2: I got an ATAR of 82. Am I guaranteed a place in the BIT?
column3: An ATAR of 82 is above the Semester 1, 2026 minimum entry score of 81.9, but meeting the minimum does not guarantee admission. Entry is competitive, and scores are reviewed annually, so check study.uq.edu.au for the most current information.

column2: What is the minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Business Management / IT dual degree?
column3: The minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Business Management / Information Technology for Semester 1, 2026 was 86.4. Entry scores are reviewed annually, so confirm current requirements at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What is the minimum entry score for the BE(Hons) / IT dual degree?
column3: The minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / Information Technology for Semester 1, 2026 was 84. Entry scores are reviewed annually in April.

column2: Do I need to study a specific maths subject in Year 12 to get into BIT?
column3: Yes, BIT has a mathematics prerequisite for entry. You should confirm the specific subject requirement with QTAC or check study.uq.edu.au, as prerequisite subjects vary by state curriculum.

column2: If I live in New South Wales, do I still apply through QTAC for BIT?
column3: Yes. All domestic students, including those from interstate states such as New South Wales, apply for BIT through QTAC. Your Year 12 results will be converted to an equivalent entry score.

column2: Is BIT available to start in Semester 2, or only Semester 1?
column3: The standard Bachelor of Information Technology is available for Semester 1 intake. For Semester 2 availability, check study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ Admissions for the most current information.

column2: I finished Year 12 in Victoria. Will my VCE results be accepted for BIT entry?
column3: Yes. VCE results from Victoria are accepted and will be converted to an equivalent entry score by QTAC. You should also confirm that your subjects meet the prerequisite requirements for BIT.

column2: Can I apply for BIT if I completed the IB (International Baccalaureate) in Australia?
column3: Yes. The International Baccalaureate is an accepted qualification for entry into UQ programs including BIT. Your IB results will be converted to an equivalent entry score, and you should confirm prerequisite requirements with QTAC or UQ Admissions.

column2: Does UQ accept mature age applicants for the BIT who haven't completed Year 12?
column3: UQ does consider alternative entry pathways for applicants who have not completed Year 12. For details on mature age entry options, visit study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ Admissions directly.

column2: Are there any subject prerequisites I need to meet before applying to BIT as a domestic student?
column3: Yes, BIT has prerequisite subject requirements, including a mathematics subject. You should verify the specific prerequisites at study.uq.edu.au or through QTAC to ensure your Year 12 subjects qualify.

column2: What happens if my ATAR is below 81.9 - is there any other way to get into BIT?
column3: If your ATAR is below the minimum entry score, you may be able to explore alternative pathways such as a UQ diploma program or other entry options. Visit study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ Admissions to discuss your specific situation.

column2: How often are entry scores for BIT updated?
column3: Entry scores for BIT and other UQ programs are reviewed annually in April. Always check study.uq.edu.au for the most current entry requirements before applying.

column2: If I apply through QTAC, what is the application process like for BIT?
column3: Domestic students submit their application through QTAC at qtac.edu.au, listing their preferred programs including BIT (QTAC code 733001). QTAC processes Year 12 results and notifies applicants of offers. Contact QTAC directly for guidance on application deadlines and steps.

column2: Is there a difference between the entry scores for the BIT compared to the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree?
column3: Yes. For Semester 1, 2026, the BIT had a minimum entry score of 81.9, while the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree required a minimum of 84. Entry scores are reviewed annually.

column2: I completed Year 12 in Western Australia. How do my WACE results get assessed for BIT entry?
column3: WACE results from Western Australia are converted to an equivalent entry score by QTAC for domestic applicants. You apply through QTAC and should confirm that your subjects meet BIT prerequisite requirements.

column2: What was the highest entry score recorded for BIT in Semester 1, 2026?
column3: The highest entry score recorded for the Bachelor of Information Technology in Semester 1, 2026 was 95.45.

column2: Can I defer my offer for BIT and start in a later semester?
column3: Deferral options depend on UQ's current policies and the specific offer received. You should contact UQ Admissions or check study.uq.edu.au for details on deferral eligibility and procedures.

column2: If I want to study the BIT/Arts dual degree, does it include an IT major?
column3: No. From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major. Dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program, and majors are no longer possible in dual degrees.

column2: Do domestic BIT students need to demonstrate English proficiency as part of their application?
column3: Domestic students who completed Year 12 in Australia typically satisfy English requirements through their senior secondary results. If your schooling was completed in a non-English context, contact UQ Admissions to confirm what evidence of English proficiency is required.

column2: I am an international student. What English proficiency score do I need for the BIT?
column3: International students must meet UQ's English language requirements. Accepted tests include IELTS, TOEFL iBT, and PTE Academic. Check study.uq.edu.au for the minimum scores required for the BIT program.

column2: Do I need to sit an IELTS test if English is my first language?
column3: If you are from a recognised English-speaking country or have completed sufficient study in English, you may be exempt from the English proficiency requirement. Check study.uq.edu.au to confirm whether your background qualifies for an exemption.

column2: I completed my secondary schooling in India under the CBSE. Do I meet the English requirement for BIT?
column3: Yes. A grade of 65 per cent or better in English from the CBSE satisfies UQ's Minimum English requirement. You should also confirm you meet the mathematics prerequisite for BIT.

column2: What is the application fee I need to pay as an international student applying to BIT?
column3: A non-refundable application fee of AUD $150 applies to all international applications, regardless of how you submit your application.

column2: As an international student on a student visa, how many hours can I work per fortnight during semester?
column3: From 1 July 2023, international students on a student visa can work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester.

column2: I am from China. Will my high school results be accepted for entry into BIT?
column3: UQ accepts a range of international secondary qualifications for entry. You should check study.uq.edu.au to confirm whether your specific Chinese qualification is recognised and what the equivalent entry requirements are.

column2: What is the total indicative tuition fee for the BIT as an international student?
column3: The indicative total fee for the 3-year BIT is approximately AUD $201,774, based on the 2026 first-year fee of AUD $58,056. Fees are reviewed annually.

column2: What is the international tuition fee per year for BIT in 2026?
column3: The 2026 international tuition fee for the BIT is AUD $58,056 per year. Fees are subject to annual review.

column2: Is there a mathematics prerequisite for the BIT, and does it apply to international students too?
column3: Yes, there is a mathematics prerequisite for BIT that applies to all applicants, including international students. Check study.uq.edu.au for the specific subject and level required.

column2: What is the deadline for international students to apply for the BIT Honours in Semester 1?
column3: The application deadline for international students applying for BIT Honours in Semester 1 is 30 November.

column2: What is the deadline for international students to apply for the BIT Honours in Semester 2?
column3: The application deadline for international students applying for BIT Honours in Semester 2 is 31 May.

column2: What is the minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Commerce / IT for international applicants?
column3: The minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Commerce / IT is 84.4. Entry scores are reviewed annually, so check study.uq.edu.au for the most current information.

column2: If I am an international student, how much should I budget for living costs in Brisbane?
column3: Off-campus living costs are estimated at AUD $2,026 to $4,151 per month. On-campus residential college accommodation ranges from AUD $2,635 to $4,168 per month, with meals included.

column2: Is the BIT accredited by a professional body that international employers would recognise?
column3: Yes. The BIT is accredited by the Australian Computer Society (ACS), and UQ states that its qualifications are internationally recognised, meaning graduates can work anywhere in the world.

column2: What is the SSAF fee I will need to pay as an international BIT student in 2026?
column3: The Student Services and Amenities Fee (SSAF) has a maximum annual fee of AUD $373 for 2026.

column2: I completed a foundation program overseas. Can I use that to apply for the BIT instead of my high school results?
column3: UQ may accept certain foundation programs as an alternative pathway for entry into the BIT. Check study.uq.edu.au to confirm whether your specific foundation program is recognised and what entry requirements apply.

column2: How many years does the Bachelor of Information Technology take to complete full-time?
column3: The BIT is a 3-year full-time program requiring 48 units in total. A standard full-time load is 8 units per semester, which equals 16 units per year.

column2: When does Semester 1 2026 start at UQ?
column3: Semester 1, 2026 commences on 23 February 2026. Check the UQ academic calendar at study.uq.edu.au for key dates including census dates and exam periods.

column2: When does Semester 2 2026 start?
column3: Semester 2, 2026 commences on 27 July 2026. Confirm all relevant dates by consulting the UQ academic calendar at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What is a standard full-time study load per semester in the BIT?
column3: A standard full-time study load is 8 units per semester. Over two semesters this gives 16 units per year, and across 3 years you accumulate the 48 units required to graduate.

column2: If I study part-time, how long will it take me to finish the BIT?
column3: Part-time study is defined as fewer than 6 units per semester, which is less than 75% of the standard full-time load. Studying part-time will extend the BIT beyond the standard 3 years.

column2: Does the BIT have a single major or can I do multiple majors?
column3: The BIT is a single-major program. From 2026, students in dual degrees may only complete 1 major toward the single program, and a standard 3-year BIT does not typically accommodate a formal second major within its 48 units.

column2: What IT majors are available in the standalone BIT program?
column3: The BIT offers majors including Computer Science, Data Science, Software Engineering, Games Technology, and Human-Computer Interaction, among others. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the full and current list of available majors.

column2: Can I do an honours year after completing the standard 3-year BIT?
column3: Yes. The Bachelor of Information Technology (Honours) is a separate program available after your BIT. You need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale to be eligible.

column2: How long does the BIT Honours program take?
column3: The BIT (Honours) is a 1-year full-time program that follows the completion of the standard 3-year BIT. Check study.uq.edu.au for full entry and program details.

column2: When is the application deadline for BIT Honours if I am an international student applying for Semester 1?
column3: The BIT (Honours) application deadline for international students applying for Semester 1 is 30 November. Domestic students applying for Semester 1 have until 31 January.

column2: What is the application deadline for BIT Honours for a domestic student starting in Semester 2?
column3: Domestic students applying for the BIT (Honours) with a Semester 2 start have a deadline of 30 June. International students applying for Semester 2 must apply by 31 May.

column2: Does the BIT include elective units, or is every course compulsory?
column3: The BIT includes both core compulsory courses and elective units. Electives give you flexibility to explore areas beyond your major. Consult Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the breakdown of core and elective requirements.

column2: Where can I find the suggested course sequence for my BIT major semester by semester?
column3: Study plans with suggested semester-by-semester course sequences are available from the School of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science (EECS) website at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: How many units per year do I study in the BIT if I am full-time?
column3: Full-time BIT students study 16 units per year, typically 8 units per semester across two semesters. This pace allows you to complete the 48-unit program in 3 years.

column2: What is the structure of a BIT dual degree - how long does it take?
column3: BIT dual degrees are longer than the standalone 3-year BIT, with duration varying by combination. For example, the BE(Hons)/IT is a 5-year program. Check study.uq.edu.au for the specific duration of each dual degree option.

column2: From 2026, can I complete two IT majors in a BIT dual degree?
column3: No. From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program, and majors are no longer possible in dual degrees.

column2: Does the BIT cover areas like social media and mobile computing as part of the curriculum?
column3: Yes. Social and Mobile Computing is listed as one of the key areas of study within the BIT curriculum.

column2: Can I start the BIT in Semester 2, or is it only available in Semester 1?
column3: The standalone BIT is primarily a Semester 1 intake program. The BIT (Honours) is available for both Semester 1 and Semester 2 intakes. Check study.uq.edu.au to confirm current intake availability for the standard BIT.

column2: What happens to my program structure if I fail a core course in the BIT?
column3: Failing a core course may affect your ability to progress to later courses that have it as a prerequisite, which could extend your time to graduation. Seek advice from a UQ academic advisor promptly if this occurs.

column2: Is there a work integrated learning or industry placement component in the BIT?
column3: The BIT curriculum emphasises employability and industry connections through UQ's Enhance Your Employability program, including internships and industry events. For specific placement or work-integrated learning requirements, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Can I add the Concurrent Diploma in Languages to my BIT without extending my study by years?
column3: Yes. The Concurrent Diploma in Languages is 16 units and is designed to be studied alongside your BIT without significantly extending your total study time. You can apply for it after enrolling in the BIT.

column2: What is the difference between the BIT and the BIT (Honours) in terms of program length and entry requirements?
column3: The standard BIT is a 3-year program requiring 48 units, while the BIT (Honours) is an additional 1-year program you can undertake after the BIT, requiring a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale.

column2: If I want to study IT alongside Business Management, what is the structure of that dual degree?
column3: The Bachelor of Business Management / Information Technology (QTAC code 710401) is a dual degree combining both disciplines. Duration and structure details are available at study.uq.edu.au, and from 2026 students complete 1 major toward the single program only.

column2: What majors can I choose in the BIT/Arts dual degree from 2026?
column3: From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major. You select one Arts major, and majors are no longer possible within the dual degree component. Check study.uq.edu.au for the available Arts majors.

column2: I want to study both computing and business. Which dual degree lets me do that and what IT majors are available?
column3: The Bachelor of Business Management/IT or the Bachelor of Commerce/IT are both options. From 2026, dual degree students may only complete one major toward the single program, and majors are no longer possible in dual degrees. Confirm current major availability at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What IT majors are available in the BIT/Business Management dual degree?
column3: From 2026, dual degree students may only complete one major toward the single program, and majors are no longer possible in dual degrees. For current details on the BBusMgt/IT structure, check study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Can I study a Data Science major in the BIT?
column3: Yes, Data Science is available as a major in the Bachelor of Information Technology. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the required courses and study plan.

column2: If I am enrolled in the BIT, can I switch my major partway through my degree?
column3: It may be possible to change your major, but this can affect your study plan and progression. You should seek academic advice from EECS or Student Services before making any changes to confirm how it affects your course requirements.

column2: Does the BIT allow me to choose a second major within the 3-year program?
column3: The BIT is a 3-year single-major program, and its standard 48-unit structure typically does not accommodate a formal second major. If you want broader coverage, a dual degree or strategic use of electives may help, so seek academic advice.

column2: What Arts majors can I pair with IT in the BIT/Arts dual degree?
column3: The BIT/Arts dual degree allows you to choose from a range of Arts majors, including Psychology. Check study.uq.edu.au for the full list of available Arts majors for your commencement year.

column2: Is Computer Science a major in the BIT, or is it a separate degree?
column3: Computer Science is available as a major within the Bachelor of Information Technology. It is also offered as part of other UQ programs. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for full details.

column2: What majors are offered in the Bachelor of Commerce component of the BCom/IT dual degree?
column3: The Commerce component of the BCom/IT offers majors in Accounting, Business Analytics, Business Information Systems, and Finance.

column2: From 2026, can I complete two majors if I enrol in a BIT dual degree?
column3: No. From 2026, dual degree students may only complete one major toward the single program, and majors are no longer possible in dual degrees.

column2: Does the BIT/Engineering dual degree allow me to choose an IT major?
column3: From 2026, majors are no longer possible in dual degrees, so you may only complete one major toward the single program component. Check study.uq.edu.au for the current structure of the BE(Hons)/IT.

column2: What is the Human-Computer Interaction area of study in the BIT, and is it a full major?
column3: Human-computer interaction is listed as a key area of study within the BIT curriculum. For whether it constitutes a formal named major, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for your commencement year.

column2: I am interested in cybersecurity. Is there a cybersecurity major or specialisation in the BIT?
column3: Cybersecurity-related content is part of the BIT curriculum. For whether a dedicated cybersecurity major or specialisation is formally offered, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for your commencement year.

column2: Are the BIT majors the same for both domestic and international students?
column3: Yes, the same majors within the BIT program are available to both domestic and international students. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au to confirm current major offerings.

column2: Do I need to declare my major when I first enrol in the BIT?
column3: You should confirm the enrolment process and when major selection is required with UQ Admissions or your academic advisor. Check study.uq.edu.au or contact EECS at eecs.uq.edu.au for guidance.

column2: Can I complete a major in Artificial Intelligence within the BIT?
column3: For current information on whether an Artificial Intelligence major is formally available in the BIT, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au, as program structures are reviewed annually.

column2: What is the recommended way to choose a BIT major if I am unsure which one suits me?
column3: You can review study plans at eecs.uq.edu.au and Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au to compare majors. Speaking with an academic advisor at EECS is also a good way to match your interests and career goals to the right major.

column2: Are elective units in the BIT linked to a specific major, or can I choose freely?
column3: The BIT includes both major-specific required courses and elective units. The flexibility of your elective choices depends on your major. Check your major's study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for details.

column2: If I choose the Computer Science major in the BIT, which level-1 courses do I typically start with?
column3: Study plans for each BIT major, including Computer Science, are available at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au, where you can see the recommended semester-by-semester course sequence.

column2: I heard majors in BIT dual degrees changed from 2026. What exactly changed?
column3: From 2026, dual degree students may only complete one major toward the single program, and majors are no longer possible in dual degrees. This is a change from previous arrangements where dual degree students could complete majors in both programs.

column2: Does completing a specific BIT major affect my eligibility for the BIT Honours year?
column3: Eligibility for the BIT (Honours) requires a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale, regardless of your chosen major. Check study.uq.edu.au for any additional major-specific requirements that may apply.

column2: What is CSSE1001 and is it required for the BIT?
column3: CSSE1001 is Introduction to Software Engineering, which is a foundational programming course in the BIT. It is a prerequisite for many higher-level computing courses, so it is typically completed in your first semester.

column2: Does the BIT include any mathematics courses?
column3: Yes. The BIT includes mathematics courses as part of the core curriculum. You should review your specific major's study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for the exact courses required.

column2: When should I choose my BIT major - before I start or after I enrol?
column3: You typically select your major when you apply or shortly after enrolment. You should confirm the selection deadline with UQ Admissions or your academic advisor, as some first-year courses are common across majors regardless of your choice.

column2: What is DECO2500 and would it be useful for a BIT student?
column3: DECO2500 is Human-Computer Interaction, which covers user experience design, usability evaluation, and interface prototyping. It is highly relevant to BIT students interested in designing effective digital products and systems.

column2: Are there any networking or cybersecurity courses in the BIT?
column3: Yes. The BIT curriculum includes areas relevant to networks and security depending on your major. Check the study plan for your chosen major at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for specific course offerings.

column2: What does the Data Science major in the BIT cover?
column3: The Data Science major covers topics such as data analysis, machine learning, statistical methods, and data visualisation. Full course details and the study plan are available at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses.

column2: How many elective units do I get in the 3-year BIT?
column3: The BIT requires 48 units to graduate over 3 years, and the number of free elective units depends on your major. Check your specific major's study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for the exact breakdown.

column2: Can I take courses from other faculties as electives in the BIT?
column3: Yes. BIT students can typically use elective units to take courses from other faculties, subject to availability and enrolment conditions. Consult your academic advisor or check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au to confirm eligibility.

column2: What is the Games Technology major in the BIT and what kind of courses does it include?
column3: The Games Technology major focuses on the design and development of interactive digital games, including graphics programming, game engines, and game design principles. Full course details are available in the study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: Is CSSE2010 a required course in the BIT?
column3: CSSE2010 is Introduction to Computer Systems, which covers computer architecture and low-level programming. Whether it is required depends on your specific major - check your major's study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au for confirmation.

column2: Does the BIT include any teamwork or group project courses?
column3: Yes. Several BIT courses incorporate team-based projects and exercises, including DECO1400 which features both individual and team-based activities. Group work is a recurring component across the degree.

column2: What is INFS3202 and is it part of the BIT?
column3: INFS3202 is Web Information Systems, which covers the design and development of web-based information systems. Whether it forms part of your BIT program depends on your major - check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for details.

column2: Can I get credit for courses I completed at another university before starting the BIT?
column3: Yes. UQ offers credit transfer and recognition of prior learning for relevant prior study. You should apply through UQ Admissions and provide official transcripts - visit study.uq.edu.au for the credit application process.

column2: What first-year courses are common to most BIT majors?
column3: Most BIT majors share foundational first-year courses such as CSSE1001 (Introduction to Software Engineering) and introductory mathematics or information systems courses. Check the study plan for your specific major at eecs.uq.edu.au for the full first-year sequence.

column2: Is there a suggested order I should complete my BIT courses in?
column3: Yes. Study plans providing a suggested semester-by-semester course sequence are available from the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses. Following the study plan helps ensure you meet prerequisites on time.

column2: What is the Information Systems major in the BIT focused on?
column3: The Information Systems major focuses on how organisations design, implement, and manage technology-based information systems to support business processes. Full course details and the study plan are available at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: Can I change my BIT major after I have already started the degree?
column3: It may be possible to change your major after commencing, but this could affect your study plan and time to completion. You should speak with an academic advisor in EECS or contact UQ Student Services before making any changes.

column2: Does the BIT include any industry placement or internship courses?
column3: The BIT does include practical and industry-connected learning opportunities. UQ's Enhance Your Employability program also supports internships. Check eecs.uq.edu.au and study.uq.edu.au for specific placement course options within your major.

column2: Are the courses in the BIT the same regardless of which semester I start in?
column3: The core courses and study plan are generally consistent, but course availability may differ slightly between Semester 1 and Semester 2 intakes. Check the study plan for your major at eecs.uq.edu.au to confirm scheduling for your intended start semester.

column2: How long does it take to complete the BIT / Arts dual degree?
column3: The Bachelor of Information Technology / Arts is a 4-year full-time program. Check study.uq.edu.au for the exact unit requirements and study plan.

column2: What is the minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / IT dual degree?
column3: The minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / IT for Semester 1, 2026 is 84. Check study.uq.edu.au for the most current entry requirements.

column2: Does the BIT / Arts dual degree include an IT major from 2026?
column3: No. From 2026, the BIT / Arts dual degree does not include an IT major. Students may complete 1 major toward the single program, and majors are no longer possible in dual degrees.

column2: I want to study both IT and business. Should I choose BBusMgt/IT or BCom/IT?
column3: Both combine IT with a business discipline, but they differ in focus. BBusMgt/IT suits students interested in management and organisational leadership, while BCom/IT is better suited to those interested in accounting, finance, or business analytics. Review the course structures at study.uq.edu.au to decide which aligns with your goals.

column2: What majors are available in the BIT component of the BIT / Arts dual degree?
column3: From 2026, majors are no longer possible in dual degrees. Students in the BIT / Arts dual degree may only complete 1 major toward the single program component. Check study.uq.edu.au for the current program structure.

column2: Can I complete two majors in a BIT dual degree from 2026?
column3: No. From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program. Majors are no longer possible in dual degrees.

column2: What Arts majors can I choose in the BIT / Arts dual degree?
column3: The BIT / Arts dual degree allows you to select from the range of Arts majors offered by UQ, including Psychology. Check study.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for the full list of available Arts majors.

column2: What is the QTAC code for the Bachelor of Information Technology / Engineering (Honours) dual degree?
column3: The QTAC code for the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / IT is 733101. Domestic students apply through QTAC at qtac.edu.au.

column2: How long does the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / IT dual degree take to complete?
column3: The Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / IT is a 5-year full-time program. Check study.uq.edu.au for the full program structure and unit requirements.

column2: Can I apply for a BIT dual degree as an international student?
column3: Yes. International students can apply for BIT dual degrees, including the BE(Hons)/IT. You apply directly through UQ's international admissions portal. A non-refundable application fee of AUD $150 applies.

column2: Are the indicative tuition fees the same for a BIT dual degree as for the standalone BIT?
column3: Indicative fees for dual degrees may differ from the standalone BIT because they incorporate courses from two disciplines. Check study.uq.edu.au for the indicative fees specific to each dual degree program.

column2: Do BIT dual degree students get a Commonwealth Supported Place?
column3: Yes. Domestic students in Commonwealth Supported Places across all UQ undergraduate programs, including dual degrees, can access HECS-HELP as long as they meet all eligibility requirements.

column2: What IT majors can I choose in the BE(Hons) / IT dual degree?
column3: From 2026, majors are no longer possible in dual degrees. Students may only complete 1 major toward the single program. Check study.uq.edu.au for the current structure of the BE(Hons)/IT program.

column2: What is the CRICOS code for the Bachelor of Business Management / Information Technology?
column3: The CRICOS code for the Bachelor of Business Management / Information Technology is 093912F. Confirm current details at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Will completing a BIT dual degree take longer than the standalone BIT?
column3: Yes. BIT dual degrees take longer than the standalone 3-year BIT. For example, the BIT/Arts is 4 years and the BE(Hons)/IT is 5 years full-time.

column2: Are BIT dual degrees accredited by the Australian Computer Society?
column3: The standalone BIT is accredited by the Australian Computer Society (ACS). For accreditation details specific to each dual degree, check study.uq.edu.au or contact the School of EECS.

column2: Can I add a Concurrent Diploma in Languages to a BIT dual degree?
column3: The Concurrent Diploma in Languages can generally be studied alongside UQ bachelor's programs. Whether it is available alongside a specific dual degree should be confirmed with UQ Admissions, as the additional study load may be a consideration.

column2: Do BIT dual degree students follow a different study plan to standalone BIT students?
column3: Yes. Each dual degree has its own study plan that integrates courses from both disciplines. Suggested study plans are available from the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses.

column2: I am a domestic student in a Commonwealth Supported Place. How much will I pay per year for the BIT?
column3: Domestic students in Commonwealth Supported Places pay a government-subsidised contribution rate, not the full tuition fee. For precise annual figures, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au, as fees depend on your specific courses and are reviewed annually.

column2: What is the annual domestic tuition fee for the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree?
column3: The approximate annual domestic tuition fee for the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / IT is AUD $8,285 per year based on 16 units, indicative for 2026. Fees are reviewed annually.

column2: If I withdraw from my BIT courses after the census date, will I get a refund?
column3: After the census date, refunds are only considered in special circumstances. If you cancel your enrolment by the census date, fees can be refunded in full for continuing students, though commencing students may incur an administrative charge. Refer to the Student Refund Procedures at policies.uq.edu.au for full details.

column2: Can I use HECS-HELP to defer my tuition fees as a domestic BIT student?
column3: Yes. Domestic students in Commonwealth Supported Places, including those in BIT and dual degrees, can access HECS-HELP to defer their tuition fees, provided you meet all Commonwealth supported place eligibility requirements.

column2: How much money do I need to show for a student visa to cover living costs?
column3: When applying for an Australian student visa, you must demonstrate evidence of at least AUD $29,710 to cover minimum living costs.

column2: What are the expected monthly living costs if I rent off campus in Brisbane?
column3: Off-campus living costs are estimated at AUD $2,026 to $4,151 per month. This range reflects differences in accommodation type, lifestyle, and personal spending.

column2: Is on-campus college accommodation more expensive than renting off campus?
column3: On-campus residential college fees range from AUD $2,635 to $4,168 per month including meals, while off-campus living costs range from AUD $2,026 to $4,151 per month. On-campus options can be comparable or slightly higher but include meals, which affects the overall value.

column2: Is there an application fee I need to pay as an international student applying to UQ?
column3: Yes. A non-refundable application fee of AUD $150 applies to all international applications, regardless of how the application is submitted.

column2: Do I have to pay the application fee if I apply through an approved UQ agent?
column3: Yes. The non-refundable international application fee of AUD $150 applies regardless of the submission method, including applications made through an approved UQ agent.

column2: As an international student on a student visa, how many hours can I work to help cover my living costs during semester?
column3: From 1 July 2023, international students on a student visa can work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester. This can help offset living costs while you study.

column2: Are there scholarships available for international BIT students at UQ?
column3: UQ does offer scholarships for international students, but specific scholarship names and eligibility details are not confirmed in the available source documents. Check study.uq.edu.au for the most current scholarship information.

column2: What is the minimum entry score for the BCom/IT dual degree, and how does its fee compare to the standalone BIT for international students?
column3: The minimum entry score for the BCom/IT is 84.4. International tuition fees for dual degrees differ from the standalone BIT; check study.uq.edu.au for the specific indicative fees for the BCom/IT program, as fees are reviewed annually.

column2: Will my BIT tuition fees increase each year I am studying?
column3: UQ reviews fees annually, so the fee you pay in your second or third year may differ from your first-year rate. Check study.uq.edu.au each year or contact UQ Admissions for the latest fee schedule.

column2: What is the approximate annual domestic tuition fee for the BBusMgt/IT dual degree?
column3: Specific annual domestic tuition fee figures for the BBusMgt/IT are not confirmed in the available source documents. Check study.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for the current indicative fees.

column2: Does the SSAF cover things like the student union, sporting facilities, or legal support?
column3: The SSAF funds non-academic student services, which can include services such as the student union and related support. The maximum annual SSAF for 2026 is AUD $373; for a detailed breakdown of what it covers, refer to uq.edu.au.

column2: If I take a leave of absence from my BIT, do I still have to pay fees?
column3: Fee obligations during a leave of absence depend on the timing relative to the census date and your specific circumstances. Refer to the Student Refund Procedures at policies.uq.edu.au or contact UQ Student Services for guidance.

column2: Are the tuition fees the same for the BIT (Honours) as for the standard 3-year BIT for international students?
column3: The 2026 international tuition fee is AUD $58,056 per year. Whether this rate applies identically to BIT (Honours) is not confirmed in the available source documents - check study.uq.edu.au for BIT (Honours) fee details.

column2: I am planning to add the Concurrent Diploma in Languages to my BIT. Will this cost extra?
column3: Adding the Concurrent Diploma in Languages involves additional units of study, which may incur additional fees. For specific cost details, check study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ Admissions.

column2: How do I pay my tuition fees as an international student at UQ?
column3: Specific payment methods and due dates are not detailed in the available source documents. Contact UQ Student Fees or visit my.uq.edu.au for information on payment options and deadlines.

column2: Does UQ charge different tuition fees depending on which BIT major I choose?
column3: Tuition fees for the BIT are generally set at the program level rather than by individual major. For precise fee details by major or course, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What is the total indicative fee for an international student completing the full BE(Hons)/IT dual degree?
column3: A specific total indicative fee for the full BE(Hons)/IT dual degree for international students is not confirmed in the available source documents. Check study.uq.edu.au for the most current indicative fee information.

column2: Does UQ have clubs or societies specifically for IT and computing students?
column3: Yes. UQ has over 220 clubs and societies, including technology, coding, gaming, and entrepreneurship clubs that are relevant to IT and computing students.

column2: What legal support is available to me as a UQ student if I run into problems?
column3: The UQ Student Union (UQU) can assist with legal or migration support. Contact UQU directly for more details on the services available.

column2: I am an international BIT student. How many hours can I work per week on a student visa?
column3: From 1 July 2023, student visa holders can work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester. Check the Department of Home Affairs website for the most current visa work conditions.

column2: What is the Student Services and Amenities Fee (SSAF) and how much is it?
column3: The SSAF is a compulsory fee that funds student services and amenities such as clubs, sports, and support programs. The maximum annual SSAF fee for 2026 is AUD $373.

column2: Does UQ provide career support specifically for IT students?
column3: Yes. UQ provides career services through the Enhance Your Employability program, including internships, career workshops, research projects, and industry events. The EAIT faculty also has industry connections through research partnerships and employer networks relevant to IT careers.

column2: I am feeling overwhelmed with study. What student support services does UQ offer?
column3: UQ offers a range of student support services including counselling, academic skills assistance, and wellbeing programs. Visit study.uq.edu.au for a full list of available support services.

column2: Are UQ sports and fitness facilities available to BIT students?
column3: Yes. UQ has on-campus sports and fitness facilities available to all enrolled students. These are partly supported through the SSAF, which all students pay each year.

column2: Can I add a language diploma to my BIT to improve my employment prospects?
column3: Yes. The Concurrent Diploma in Languages lets you study one of 9 available languages alongside your BIT. It is 16 units and adds valuable language skills to your IT qualification. Domestic students can apply at study.uq.edu.au/study-options/programs/diploma-languages-1602.

column2: What is the UQ Student Union and what does it do?
column3: The UQ Student Union (UQU) is the student representative body that provides services including legal and migration support, clubs and societies, events, and advocacy for students. It is a key part of campus life at UQ.

column2: Is there support available for students who are struggling with English academic writing?
column3: Yes. UQ provides academic skills support services to help students with English writing, referencing, and study techniques. Check study.uq.edu.au for details on how to access these services.

column2: How do I prove I have enough money to support myself when applying for an Australian student visa?
column3: When applying for a student visa, you need to demonstrate a minimum of AUD $29,710 in living cost evidence. This is a Department of Home Affairs requirement, so check their website for the full list of accepted evidence types.

column2: Are there global study or exchange opportunities available to BIT students?
column3: Yes. Global study is one of the options available through UQ's Enhance Your Employability program, allowing BIT students to gain international experience as part of their degree. Check study.uq.edu.au for details on available programs.

column2: What is the difference in monthly cost between living on-campus and off-campus?
column3: On-campus residential colleges cost AUD $2,635 to $4,168 per month with meals included, while off-campus living is estimated at AUD $2,026 to $4,151 per month. The best option depends on your priorities around convenience, community, and budget.

column2: Does UQ have support services for international students adjusting to life in Australia?
column3: Yes. UQ provides support services for international students including orientation programs, wellbeing support, and legal or migration assistance through UQU. Visit study.uq.edu.au for a full overview of available services.

column2: Can I get help finding an approved UQ agent in my country before I apply?
column3: Yes. UQ works with approved agents worldwide to assist international students with applications. You can find a list of approved agents at study.uq.edu.au/contact/find-approved-uq-agent-near-you.

column2: Is there an industry internship program I can join as a BIT student at UQ?
column3: Yes. Internships are available through UQ's Enhance Your Employability program, and the EAIT faculty has industry connections through research partnerships and employer networks that can support IT students seeking work experience.

column2: Are UQ IT graduates able to work internationally after completing their degree?
column3: Yes. UQ's Engineering, Computer Science and IT faculty states that qualifications are internationally recognised and graduates can work anywhere in the world.

column2: What does the Enhance Your Employability program include for BIT students?
column3: The program includes options such as global study, internships, career workshops, research projects, and industry events. These experiences are designed to strengthen your employability alongside your IT degree.

column2: Is the SSAF mandatory, or can I choose not to pay it?
column3: The SSAF is a compulsory fee for all enrolled UQ students. For 2026, the maximum annual amount is AUD $373, and it funds student services including clubs, sports, and support programs.

column2: What kind of technology or coding clubs exist at UQ for BIT students to get involved with?
column3: UQ has over 220 clubs and societies, including technology, coding, gaming, and entrepreneurship clubs relevant to IT and computing students. Check the UQU website for a current list of active clubs.

column2: If I am a domestic part-time BIT student, will I still have access to the same campus support services?
column3: Yes. All enrolled UQ students, including part-time domestic students, have access to campus support services such as counselling, academic skills support, and student union services. Visit study.uq.edu.au to explore what is available.

column2: Does the BIT prepare me for a career in cybersecurity?
column3: Yes. The BIT includes cybersecurity as a key area of study and offers a dedicated major in Cybersecurity, which prepares graduates for roles in information security, risk management, and network defence. For specific course details, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: I want to work in data science after graduation. Is the BIT a good fit?
column3: Yes. The BIT offers a Data Science major covering areas such as data analytics, machine learning, and statistical reasoning, which are directly relevant to data science careers. UQ also provides career services through its Enhance Your Employability program to help you connect with industry.

column2: Can I work as a software engineer after completing the BIT?
column3: Yes. Software engineering and development is one of the most common career pathways for BIT graduates, supported by core programming courses such as CSSE1001 and CSSE2002 and majors like Software Engineering. The BIT is also accredited by the Australian Computer Society, which strengthens your professional standing.

column2: Is the BIT degree accredited by any professional body?
column3: Yes. The Bachelor of Information Technology is accredited by the Australian Computer Society (ACS), which is widely recognised by employers and professional bodies across Australia and internationally.

column2: What does ACS accreditation mean for my career prospects?
column3: ACS accreditation means your degree meets nationally recognised professional standards for IT, which many employers use as a quality benchmark when hiring. It can also support pathways to professional membership of the ACS, which is valued in the industry.

column2: If I study the BIT at UQ, can I work overseas after graduation?
column3: Yes. UQ's Engineering, Computer Science and IT faculty states that its qualifications are internationally recognised, meaning BIT graduates can pursue careers anywhere in the world.

column2: Does UQ help BIT students find internships or work experience?
column3: Yes. UQ offers career support through the Enhance Your Employability program, which includes internships, industry events, and career workshops. The EAIT faculty also has industry connections through research partnerships and employer networks relevant to IT careers.

column2: What career support services are available to me as a BIT student?
column3: UQ provides career services through its Enhance Your Employability program, covering global study, internships, career workshops, research projects, and industry events. The EAIT faculty also maintains industry connections that can help you access IT-specific opportunities.

column2: Can I work part-time in IT while studying BIT to build experience?
column3: Yes. Domestic students can work without restriction, while international students on a student visa can work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester. Gaining part-time IT work alongside your studies is a practical way to build industry experience before graduation.

column2: Should I choose the Data Science major or the Cybersecurity major if I want better job prospects?
column3: Both majors lead to strong career prospects in high-demand fields. Data Science suits roles in analytics, machine learning, and business intelligence, while Cybersecurity targets information security and risk management careers. Your choice should reflect your personal interests and long-term career goals - consult an academic adviser if you are unsure.

column2: Does doing a dual degree like BCom/IT improve my career outcomes compared to a standalone BIT?
column3: A dual degree such as the BCom/IT can expand your career options by combining IT expertise with business or commerce skills, making you competitive for roles in areas like business analytics, management consulting, or fintech. However, both pathways can lead to strong IT careers - your choice depends on how broad you want your skill set to be.

column2: Is a career in IT project management possible after the BIT?
column3: Yes. Skills developed across the BIT, particularly in areas like software engineering, human-computer interaction, and systems analysis, are relevant to IT project management roles. You may also consider supplementing your degree with project management certifications after graduation.

column2: Can completing the BIT (Honours) help me get better jobs or higher salaries?
column3: The BIT (Honours) demonstrates advanced research and technical capability, which can be advantageous for senior or specialised roles and for postgraduate study pathways. For specific salary data, check resources such as Graduate Careers Australia or the ACS ICT workforce reports.

column2: What if I want to go into research or academia after the BIT - is that possible?
column3: Yes. Completing the BIT (Honours) is typically the first step toward a research career, as it provides the foundation needed for a Master of Philosophy or PhD program. UQ has strong research groups in areas such as artificial intelligence, cybersecurity, and human-computer interaction.

column2: Does the BIT include any industry projects or real-world experience built into the curriculum?
column3: The BIT curriculum incorporates team-based and industry-relevant projects across various courses, and UQ's Enhance Your Employability program supports additional industry engagement through internships and employer events. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for details on specific courses that include industry components.

column2: I am interested in working in human-computer interaction or UX design. Can the BIT lead to that?
column3: Yes. The BIT includes human-computer interaction as a key area of study, and courses such as DECO1400 (Introduction to Web Design) introduce human-centred design principles and UX concepts. These skills are directly relevant to careers in UX design and product development.

column2: Will studying the Concurrent Diploma in Languages alongside my BIT improve my career prospects?
column3: Yes. Adding a Concurrent Diploma in Languages to your BIT can strengthen your employability in international or multicultural workplaces, as it demonstrates both technical IT skills and cross-cultural communication ability. Nine languages are available through the diploma program.

column2: Can I pursue a career in artificial intelligence or machine learning with a BIT degree?
column3: Yes. The BIT includes areas such as data science, social and mobile computing, and programming that provide a foundation for AI and machine learning careers. For more specialised AI study, you may also wish to explore postgraduate options at UQ after completing your BIT.

column2: What if I want to start my own technology business after the BIT - does UQ support entrepreneurship?
column3: Yes. UQ has entrepreneurship and innovation clubs and resources relevant to technology startups, and the Enhance Your Employability program includes activities that build entrepreneurial skills. For startup-specific support, explore UQ's entrepreneurship ecosystem at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Are there global graduate outcomes for UQ IT students or just jobs in Australia?
column3: UQ's Engineering, Computer Science and IT faculty states that its qualifications are internationally recognised and graduates can work anywhere in the world, so career outcomes extend well beyond Australia.

column2: Does the BBusMgt/IT dual degree open up different career paths compared to the standalone BIT?
column3: Yes. The BBusMgt/IT combines IT skills with business management expertise, making graduates competitive for roles that sit at the intersection of technology and business leadership, such as IT management, strategy consulting, and business transformation roles.

column2: Can I become a registered or certified IT professional in Australia with a UQ BIT degree?
column3: Yes. Because the BIT is accredited by the Australian Computer Society, it can support pathways to ACS professional membership, which is a recognised form of IT professional certification in Australia. Check the ACS website for current membership requirements and categories.

column2: What types of careers does the Software Engineering major in the BIT lead to?
column3: The Software Engineering major prepares graduates for roles such as software developer, software architect, systems engineer, DevOps engineer, and quality assurance engineer, across industries ranging from fintech to healthcare and government. Check study.uq.edu.au for the full list of courses in this major.

column2: What GPA do I need to apply for the BIT Honours program?
column3: You need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale to be eligible to apply for the Bachelor of Information Technology (Honours).

column2: How long does the BIT Honours take to complete?
column3: The BIT (Honours) is a 1-year program undertaken after completing the 3-year Bachelor of Information Technology. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for full details on structure and requirements.

column2: Can I start the BIT Honours in Semester 2?
column3: Yes. The BIT (Honours) is available for both Semester 1 and Semester 2 intakes.

column2: What is the application deadline for the BIT Honours if I am an international student applying for Semester 1?
column3: The application deadline for international students applying for BIT (Honours) Semester 1 is 30 November.

column2: What is the application deadline for domestic students applying for BIT Honours in Semester 1?
column3: Domestic students applying for BIT (Honours) Semester 1 must submit their application by 31 January.

column2: I want to start BIT Honours in Semester 2. What is the deadline if I am an international student?
column3: International students applying for BIT (Honours) Semester 2 must apply by 31 May.

column2: What is the domestic application deadline for BIT Honours Semester 2?
column3: Domestic students applying for BIT (Honours) Semester 2 must submit their application by 30 June.

column2: Is the BIT Honours a separate program from the standard BIT?
column3: Yes. The Bachelor of Information Technology (Honours) is a separate 1-year program that you apply for after completing the 3-year BIT, provided you meet the minimum GPA requirement of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale.

column2: What does the BIT Honours program involve?
column3: The BIT (Honours) typically involves advanced coursework and a significant research or project component. For full details on program structure, visit Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: If my GPA is 4.8 at the end of my BIT, can I still apply for Honours?
column3: No. The minimum GPA required for BIT (Honours) is 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale, so a GPA of 4.8 would not meet the eligibility requirement.

column2: Does the BIT Honours have a CRICOS code for international students?
column3: CRICOS codes are assigned to programs for international students. For the specific CRICOS code for the BIT (Honours), check study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ Admissions directly.

column2: Will completing BIT Honours improve my career prospects compared to the standard BIT?
column3: UQ states that its IT qualifications are internationally recognised and Honours-level study demonstrates advanced research and analytical skills valued by employers. For specific career outcomes, visit the EAIT faculty pages or UQ's career services.

column2: Do I need to complete 48 units before applying for BIT Honours?
column3: Yes. The standard BIT requires 48 units over 3 years, and BIT (Honours) is undertaken after completing that degree, provided you also meet the minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale.

column2: What is the QTAC code for the BIT Honours program?
column3: The specific QTAC code for the BIT (Honours) is not confirmed in the current source documents. Check study.uq.edu.au or qtac.edu.au for the correct code.

column2: Can I apply for BIT Honours while I am still completing my BIT?
column3: You should check the application timeline and eligibility requirements with UQ Admissions, as the minimum GPA of 5.0 must be met. Visit study.uq.edu.au for guidance on when to apply.

column2: Is the BIT Honours program also accredited by the Australian Computer Society?
column3: The standard BIT is accredited by the Australian Computer Society (ACS). For confirmation of whether ACS accreditation extends to the BIT (Honours), check study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ directly.

column2: What is the tuition fee for BIT Honours for international students?
column3: International tuition fees for 2026 are AUD $58,056 per year. Since Honours is a 1-year program, the indicative fee would be approximately that amount, but confirm the exact figure at study.uq.edu.au as fees are reviewed annually.

column2: When does Semester 1 2026 start if I am commencing BIT Honours?
column3: Semester 1, 2026 commences on 23 February 2026.

column2: If I start BIT Honours in Semester 2 2026, when does that semester begin?
column3: Semester 2, 2026 commences on 27 July 2026.

column2: How is the GPA calculated at UQ for Honours eligibility purposes?
column3: UQ uses a 7-point scale to calculate GPA, and you need a minimum of 5.0 to be eligible for BIT (Honours). For details on how individual course grades translate to GPA points, visit my.uq.edu.au or contact your academic advisor.

column2: Should I choose to do BIT Honours or go straight into the workforce after finishing my BIT?
column3: This depends on your career goals. Honours can strengthen your research skills and may be beneficial if you are considering postgraduate study or research-focused roles. UQ's career services can help you weigh your options.

column2: Can I study BIT Honours part-time?
column3: Part-time availability for BIT (Honours) is not confirmed in the current source documents. Contact UQ Admissions or visit study.uq.edu.au for details on study mode options.

column2: What happens if I miss the BIT Honours application deadline?
column3: If you miss a deadline, you would need to apply for the next available intake. Semester 1 international deadline is 30 November and domestic is 31 January; Semester 2 international deadline is 31 May and domestic is 30 June.

column2: Is BIT Honours available at UQ Gatton or only at the St Lucia campus?
column3: Campus availability for BIT (Honours) is not confirmed in the current source documents. Check study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ Admissions for details on where the program is delivered.

column2: I am an international student. How do I apply for the BIT at UQ?
column3: International students apply directly through UQ's online application portal at study.uq.edu.au. A non-refundable application fee of AUD $150 applies to all international applications.

column2: Can I apply for the BIT through a UQ-approved agent instead of applying directly?
column3: Yes. UQ works with approved agents worldwide who can assist with your application. You can find a list of approved agents at study.uq.edu.au/contact/find-approved-uq-agent-near-you.

column2: If I want to apply for the BIT Honours program, what GPA do I need?
column3: To apply for the Bachelor of Information Technology (Honours), you need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale. Check Programs and Courses for any additional entry requirements.

column2: What are the application deadlines for the BIT Honours program for international students?
column3: For international students, the BIT (Honours) application deadline is 30 November for Semester 1 entry and 31 May for Semester 2 entry. Apply early to allow time for documentation and visa processing.

column2: What are the application deadlines for the BIT Honours program for domestic students?
column3: For domestic students, the BIT (Honours) application deadline is 31 January for Semester 1 entry and 30 June for Semester 2 entry.

column2: I finished high school in India with a CBSE certificate. Do I meet the English requirement for the BIT?
column3: Yes. A grade of 65 per cent or better in English from the CBSE satisfies the Minimum English requirement for UQ. You should also confirm you meet the mathematics prerequisite.

column2: Do I need to meet a mathematics prerequisite to get into the BIT?
column3: Yes. The BIT has a mathematics prerequisite. Check study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ Admissions to confirm which specific mathematics subjects or levels satisfy this requirement for your qualification.

column2: If I don't meet the English language requirement, are there alternative pathways to satisfy it?
column3: UQ accepts several English proficiency tests and qualifications as evidence of English language ability. If you do not meet the standard requirement, contact UQ Admissions at study.uq.edu.au to discuss alternative pathways or foundation programs.

column2: How much money do I need to show I have available to support myself on a student visa?
column3: To obtain a student visa, you need to demonstrate living costs of at least AUD $29,710 as the minimum evidence of funds. This is separate from your tuition fees.

column2: Can I apply for the BIT if I have not yet received my final Year 12 results?
column3: Yes. You can apply before receiving final results, and many QTAC offers are made based on predicted or in-progress scores. Contact QTAC or UQ Admissions to confirm the process for your situation.

column2: I am a domestic student from New South Wales. Do I still apply through QTAC for the BIT?
column3: Yes. All domestic students, including those from interstate, apply for the BIT through QTAC at qtac.edu.au. Your interstate results will be converted to an equivalent entry score.

column2: Does applying through an approved agent cost more than applying directly to UQ?
column3: The non-refundable AUD $150 application fee applies regardless of whether you apply directly or through an approved agent. For fee details, confirm with UQ or your agent before applying.

column2: What documents do I typically need to submit when applying for the BIT as an international student?
column3: Typical documentation includes academic transcripts, English proficiency test results, and proof of identity. Check study.uq.edu.au for the complete list of required documents specific to your country and qualification.

column2: What is the QTAC code for the Bachelor of Information Technology / Business Management dual degree?
column3: The QTAC code for the Bachelor of Business Management / Information Technology is 710401.

column2: If I apply for the BIT and don't get in this semester, can I defer my application to the next intake?
column3: Deferral and reapplication options vary depending on your circumstances and the intake. Contact UQ Admissions at study.uq.edu.au to discuss your options if your application is unsuccessful.

column2: Are BIT applications open for both Semester 1 and Semester 2 each year?
column3: Yes. The BIT accepts applications for both Semester 1 (commencing 23 February 2026) and Semester 2 (commencing 27 July 2026) intakes. Check study.uq.edu.au for application opening and closing dates.

column2: What happens after I submit my international application to UQ? How long does it take to get an offer?
column3: After submission, UQ assesses your academic documents, English proficiency, and other requirements before issuing a formal offer. Processing times vary, so check study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ Admissions for indicative timeframes.

column2: What does ACS accreditation mean for my career after finishing the BIT?
column3: ACS accreditation means your degree meets nationally recognised professional standards for IT, which can support membership applications, career progression, and employer recognition in Australia and internationally.

column2: Is there an AI-focused major I can choose in the BIT?
column3: Check the current list of BIT majors at study.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses, as major offerings are reviewed periodically and specific AI-focused options may be available or introduced.

column2: What student visa work rights do I have as an international BIT student in Australia?
column3: From 1 July 2023, international students on a student visa can work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester. Different conditions may apply during scheduled course breaks.

column2: Can I go on a student exchange while studying the BIT at UQ?
column3: UQ offers exchange and study abroad opportunities for eligible students. You should visit study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ's Global Experiences team for details on how exchange fits within your BIT program requirements.

column2: Will courses I complete on exchange count toward my BIT degree?
column3: Credit for exchange courses is assessed on a case-by-case basis. You would need prior approval from your faculty to ensure exchange units count toward your BIT requirements. Contact the EECS school or student administration for guidance.

column2: What is UQ's policy on academic integrity, and what happens if I am caught cheating?
column3: UQ takes academic integrity seriously. Breaches such as plagiarism or contract cheating can result in penalties ranging from a grade of zero to exclusion from the program. Refer to UQ's Student Integrity and Misconduct policy at policies.uq.edu.au for full details.

column2: What is the difference between the Bachelor of Information Technology and the Bachelor of Computer Science at UQ?
column3: The BIT focuses on applied IT skills, industry readiness, and areas like networks, software development, and information systems, while the Bachelor of Computer Science typically has a stronger emphasis on theoretical computing and mathematical foundations. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au to compare specific course requirements for each program.

column2: Should I choose the BIT or the Bachelor of Computer Science if I want to work in software development?
column3: Both degrees can lead to software development careers, but the BIT is more industry-focused while the Bachelor of Computer Science places greater emphasis on computing theory. Review the majors and course lists for each program at study.uq.edu.au to decide which better suits your goals.

column2: What is the minimum GPA needed to apply for the BIT Honours year?
column3: You need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale to be eligible to apply for the Bachelor of Information Technology (Honours).

column2: When is the application deadline for BIT Honours if I am an international student starting in Semester 1?
column3: The application deadline for international students commencing BIT Honours in Semester 1 is 30 November.

column2: I am a domestic student wanting to start BIT Honours in Semester 2. When do I need to apply by?
column3: Domestic students applying for BIT Honours with a Semester 2 commencement must submit their application by 30 June.

column2: What is the application deadline for international students applying for BIT Honours in Semester 2?
column3: International students applying for BIT Honours with a Semester 2 commencement must apply by 31 May.

column2: I am an international student. When does Semester 1, 2026 start at UQ?
column3: Semester 1, 2026 commences on 23 February 2026. You should plan your arrival and visa arrangements accordingly.

column2: When does Semester 2, 2026 begin at UQ?
column3: Semester 2, 2026 commences on 27 July 2026. Check the UQ academic calendar at study.uq.edu.au for key dates such as census and examination periods.

column2: What is the SSAF and how much will I pay as a BIT student in 2026?
column3: The Student Services and Amenities Fee (SSAF) is a compulsory fee that funds non-academic student services. The maximum annual SSAF for 2026 is AUD $373.

column2: I am thinking about living off-campus while studying the BIT. How much should I budget per month?
column3: Off-campus living costs are estimated at AUD $2,026 to $4,151 per month, depending on your location, lifestyle, and accommodation type.

column2: Does UQ have any safeguards if I have concerns about academic misconduct by another student?
column3: UQ has formal processes for reporting academic integrity concerns. You can find information on reporting procedures through UQ's Student Integrity and Misconduct policy at policies.uq.edu.au.

column2: Can I defer my BIT offer and start in a later semester?
column3: Deferral options depend on your offer type and whether you are a domestic or international applicant. Check your offer conditions and contact UQ Admissions or visit study.uq.edu.au for guidance specific to your situation.

column2: Is UQ's BIT degree recognised in countries outside Australia?
column3: Yes. UQ's Engineering, Computer Science and IT faculty states that qualifications are internationally recognised, and graduates can work anywhere in the world.

column2: Are there any AI or technology ethics topics covered in the BIT?
column3: The BIT curriculum includes areas relevant to responsible and ethical use of technology, though specific course content varies by major and year. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the most current course listings.
